questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If the equation of the curve on reflection of the ellipse $\frac{(x-4)^2}{16}+\frac{(y-3)^2}{9}=1$ about the line $x-y-2=0$ is $16 x^2+9 y^2+k_1 x-36 y+k_2=0$, then $\frac{\left(k_1+k_2\right)}{66}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\frac{(x-4)^2}{16}+\frac{(y-3)^2}{9}=1$
any point $P$ on the curve $(4(1+\cos \theta), 3(1+\sin \theta))$.
The reflection of the point $P$ about the line $x-y-2=0$ can be found using
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{h}-4(1+\cos \theta)}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{k}-3(1+\sin \theta)}{-1}=\frac{-2(4(1+\cos \theta)-3(1+\sin \theta)-2)}{2}=-(4 \cos \theta-3 \sin \theta)+1 \\
& \mathrm{~h}=4(1+\cos \theta)-4 \cos \theta+3 \sin \theta+1=4+3 \sin \theta+1 \\
& \text { and } k=3(1+\sin \theta)+4 \cos \theta-3 \sin \theta=3+4 \cos \theta-1 \\
& \left(\frac{\mathrm{~h}-5}{3}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}-2}{4}\right)^2=1
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ equation of image $16(x-5)^2+9(y-2)^2=144$
$\begin{aligned}
& 16 x^2+9 y^2-160 x-36 y+292=0 \\
& k_1=-160 \cdot k_2=292 \\
& \Rightarrow k_1+k_2=132
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.q: On an ellipse $\frac{x^2}{64}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1$, tangents drawn at $P_1, P_2, P_3 \ldots . . P_n$ intersects the major axis at $T_1, T_2, T_3$ $\ldots . T_n$ respectively. if the value of $\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\operatorname{Arca}\left(\Delta P_i T_1 S\right) \cdot \operatorname{Area}\left(\Delta P_i T_i S^{\prime}\right)}{\left(P_1 T_i\right)^2}=18$, then $n$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {Area of } \Delta P_1 S T_1=\frac{1}{2} P_1 T_1 \times S M \\ & \text { Area of } \Delta P_1 T_1 S^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} P_1 T_1 \times S^{\prime} M^{\prime} \\ & \frac{\operatorname{Area}\left(\Delta \mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~T}_1 \mathrm{~S}\right) \cdot \operatorname{Area}\left(\Delta \mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~T}_1 \mathrm{~S}^{\prime}\right)}{\left(\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~T}_1\right)^2}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{SM} \cdot \mathrm{~S}^{\prime} \mathrm{M}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{b}^2}{4} \\ & \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\operatorname{Area}(\triangle \text { PiTiS }) \cdot \operatorname{Area}\left(\triangle \text { PiTiS }^{\prime}\right)}{(\text { PiTi })^2}=\frac{n b^2}{4} \\ & \frac{n b^2}{4}=18 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{4 \times 18}{9}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=8 \end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A ray of light emerging from the point $p(0,4)$ along the tangent of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$ and strike a circle of radius $\sqrt{65}$ units and reflected from it. The reflected ray entering inside the ellipse through the focus $(-3,0)$ and strike lower surface of ellipse and gets reflected from it. If the reflected ray returns to the point $p(0,4)$, then ordinate of the centre of circle is _______
questions.solution: Using reflection property of ellipse equation of reflected ray $S P$ is $4 x+3 y=12$ ......(1) Solving equation (1) with ellipse, we get $A \equiv\left(\frac{75}{17},-\frac{32}{17}\right)$ Equation of reflected ray $\mathrm{BS}^{\prime}$ is $16 x+63+48=0$ ....(2) Equation of incident ray PB is $y=4$....(3) Equation of bisector of (2) and (3) with negative slope gives normal to circle at 3 which is $4 x+32 y-53=0$ Point $B$ is $\left(-\frac{75}{4}, 4\right)$, using parametric equation of line ordinate of centre is 5
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $A_E$ is the area of an ellipse with an eccentricity of $e=\frac{7}{25}$ and $A_F$ is the area of the shape bounded by the set of points for which two tangents of that ellipse meet at a right angles, then $\frac{A_E}{A_F}=\frac{p}{q}$, where $p$ and $q$ are positive co-prime integers. Find $(p+q)$.
questions.solution:
Since $e=\frac{7}{25}=\frac{c}{a}, c=7 k$ and $a=25 k$ and since $a^2-b^2=c^2$ in an ellipse, $b=24 k$.
The area of an ellipse is $A=\pi a b$, so $A_E=600 \pi k^2$.
The set of points for which two tangents of any curve meet at $a$ right angle is an orthopetic, and an orthopetic for any ellipse is a circle with a radius of $\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$, and therefore an area of
$$
A=\pi\left(a^2+b^2\right) \text {, so } A_F=1201 p k^2 \text {. }
$$

Therefore, $\quad \frac{A_E}{A_F}=\frac{600 \pi k^2}{1201 \pi k^3}=\frac{600}{1201}$, so $p=600, q=1201$, and $p+q=1801$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.q: The eccentricity of the ellipse which meets the straight line $\frac{x}{7}+\frac{y}{2}=1$ on the axis of $x$ and the straight line $\frac{x}{3}-\frac{y}{5}=1$ on the axis of $y$ and whose axes lie along the axes of coordinates is $\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{\lambda}$. Find $\lambda$
questions.solution:
$\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ $\qquad...(1)$
$\frac{x}{7}+\frac{y}{2}=1$ meets $x$-axis at $A(7,0)$, line $\frac{x}{3}-\frac{y}{5}=1$ meets $y$-axis at $B(0,-5)$.
(1) passes through $A$ \& $B$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{49}{\mathrm{a}^2}+0=1,0+\frac{25}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^2=49, \mathrm{~b}^2=25 ; \mathrm{b}^2=\mathrm{a}^2\left(1-\mathrm{e}^2\right) \Rightarrow 25=49\left(1-\mathrm{e}^2\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^2=\frac{24}{49} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}=\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{7}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.q: Let the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ contains the circle $(x-1)^2+y^2=1$ and has least area. If $a^2+b^2=2 n$, then find the value of $n \in N$.
questions.solution:
Since the ellipse contains the circle
$\therefore$ Solving circle with ellipse, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
b^2 x^2+a^2\left(1-(x-1)^2\right) & =a^2 b^2 \\
\left(b^2-a^2\right) x^2+2 a^2 x-a^2 b^2 & =0 \\
D & =0 \\
4 a^4+4\left(a^2 b^2\right)\left(b^2-a^2\right) & =0 \\
a^2+b^2\left(b^2-a^2\right) & =0 \\
a^2-b^2\left(a^2 e^2\right) & =0 \\
1 & =b^2 e^2 \Rightarrow b e=1
\end{aligned}
$$

Now,
$$
\text { area of ellipse } A=\pi a b
$$
$$
\therefore
$$

Now,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& A^2=\pi^2 a^2 b^2 \\
& e^2=1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}=\frac{1}{b^2} \\
& e^2=1-\frac{1}{b^2}=\frac{b^2}{a^2} \\
& a^2=\frac{b^4}{b^2-1}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now,
$$
A^2=f(b)=\frac{b^6}{b^2-1}
$$

For maxima and minima $\quad f^{\prime}(b)=0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(b^2-1\right) 6 b^5-b^6(2 b)=0 \quad \Rightarrow 3\left(b^2-1\right)=b^2 \\
& b^2=\frac{3}{2} ; \quad \therefore \quad a^2=\frac{9}{2} \\
& \therefore \quad a^2+b^2=\frac{9}{2}+\frac{3}{2}=6=2 n \quad \Rightarrow \quad n=3 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: On the coordinate plane, ellipse $C_1: \frac{x^2}{a_1^2}+\frac{y^2}{b_1^2}=1\left(a_1>b_1>0\right)$ and hyperbola $C_2: \frac{x^2}{a_2^2}-\frac{y^2}{b_2^2}=1\left(a_2, b_2>0\right)$ has the same focus points $F_1, F_2$. Point $P$ is one of the intersection points of $C_1$ and $C_2$ and $P F_1 \perp P F_2$. If $e_1$ is the eccentricity of $C_1$ and $e_2$ is the eccentricity of $C_2$. Then find the minimum value of $9 e_1^2+e_2^2$.
questions.solution:
Given
$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 e_1 & =a_2 e_2 \\
P F_1^2+P F_2^2 & =4 a_1^2 e_1^2...(1) Using Pythagorus theorem \\
P F_1+P F_2 & =2 a_1 ...(2)\\
{\left[P F_1+P F_2\right] } & =2 a_2 ...(3)\\
(2)^2+(3)^2 & \Rightarrow 2\left[P F_1^2+P F_2^2\right]=24\left(a_1^2+a_2^2\right)...(4)
\end{aligned}
$$
From Eqns. (1) and (4)
$$
\begin{aligned}
2\left(a_1^2+a_2^2\right) & =4 a_1^2 e_1^2 \\
a_1^2+a_2^2 & =2\left(a_1^2 e_1^2\right) \\
\therefore \quad 1+\left(\frac{a_2}{a_1}\right)^2 & =2 e_1^2 \\
1+\left(\frac{e_1}{e_2}\right)^2 & =2 e_1^2
\end{aligned}
$$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{e_1^2}+\frac{1}{e_2^2}=2 \\ & \text { Let } \quad \frac{1}{e_1}=\sqrt{2} \cos \theta, \frac{1}{e_2}=\sqrt{2} \sin \theta \\ & \therefore \quad E=9 e_1^2+e_2^2=\frac{9}{2} \times \sec ^2 \theta+\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[9\left(1+\tan ^2 \theta\right)+1+\cot ^2 \theta\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[10+9 \tan ^2 \theta+\cot ^2 \theta\right] \text { minimum value }=6 \text { using } \mathrm{AM} \geq \mathrm{GM} \\ & \therefore \quad E_{\text {min. }}=8 \\ & \end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If two points are taken on the minor axis of an ellipse at the same distance from the centre as the foci, then find $\lambda$ for which the sum of the squares of the perpendiculars from these points on any tangent to the ellipse is $\lambda$ $a^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the ellipse be $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ Then, the distance of $a$ focus from the centre $=a e=a \sqrt{1-b^2 / a^2}=\sqrt{a^2-b^2}$, so that the two points of minor axis are $\mathrm{S}_1\left(0, \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2}\right)$ and $\mathrm{S}_1{ }^{\prime}\left(0,-\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2}\right)$. Now any tangent to the ellipse is $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{~m}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}$ where m is a parameter.
The sum of the squares of the perpendiculars on this tangent from the two points $S_1$ and $S_1{ }^{\prime}$ is $\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{\sqrt{a^2-b^2}-\sqrt{a^2 m^2+b^2}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{m}^2}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{-\sqrt{a^2-b^2}-\sqrt{a^2 m^2+b^2}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{m}^2}}\right)^2 \\ & =2\left(a^2-b^2+a^2 m^2+b^2\right) /\left(1+m^2\right)=2 a^2 \text { (constant) }\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Find the value of $k$ for which the point $P(2, k)$ on the ellipse $x^2+2 y^2=6$, which is nearest to the line $x+y=7$.
questions.solution:
The given equation can be written as $\frac{x^2}{6}+\frac{y^2}{3}=1$, which represents an ellipse.
Any point on this ellipse is $\mathrm{P}(\sqrt{6} \cos \theta, \sqrt{3} \sin \theta)$. The shortest distance between the ellipse and the given line is along the common normal to both.
Slope of the normal at $P$ is
$\frac{\sqrt{6} \sec \theta}{\sqrt{3} \operatorname{cosec} \theta}=\sqrt{2} \tan \theta=1$ slope of normal to the line $x+y=7$
Hence $\cos \theta=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ so that P is $(2,1)$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
